summary: Christopher Caldwell argues that Trump's decision to launch a war against Iran represents a fundamental rupture with Trumpism as a political project, despite Trump's base remaining largely supportive in polls. The episode explores tensions between populism and proceduralism, examining whether Trump represents democratic restoration or authoritarian impulses. Caldwell contends that Trumpism was built on opposition to wars and institutional overreach, and that sustained economic costs from the Iran conflict could eventually erode support. The discussion situates Trump within broader right-wing populist movements in Europe and debates whether Trump's appeal is fundamentally about policy outcomes or about a transgressive leadership style.
claims_to_note: Claim about inflation and economy: Caldwell states 'inflation went up and people were furious and they remembered the Trump economy' under Biden, implying Trump's pre-pandemic economy performed well - this relates to economic data claims that could be verified
Implicit claim about labor market effects: discussion of 'tight labor market' and 'dramatic wage growth in lower quintiles' as indicators of successful policy - economic outcome claims
Claim about tariff economic effects: assertions about tariff regimes creating manufacturing preferences 'without distorting international trade unduly' - this is contested in economic literature
policy_relevance: Iran war/military intervention - Trump administration decision to attack Iran
DEI/affirmative action executive orders - Trump dismantled diversity initiatives through executive action
DOGE (Department of Government Efficiency) - effort to restructure federal bureaucracy and civil service
Tariff policy - Trump's use of tariffs as governing mechanism outside traditional legislative process
University funding/Title IX policy - Trump administration pressure on universities regarding DEI programs
Administrative state reform - broader discussion of executive vs. bureaucratic authority
Immigration policy - mentioned as core issue in Trump and European right-wing populism
War powers/declaration of war - discussion of Trump bypassing Congressional declaration process
key_quotes: The attack on Iran is so wildly inconsistent with the wishes of his own base, so diametrically opposed to their reading of the national interest, that it is likely to mark the end of Trumpism as a project.

Trump promised a country in which you'd get the stuff you voted for and not the permanent state... He was sort of promising a return to a sort of a more 19th century state that you can criticize as being based on patronage.
To live outside the law, you must be honest. And in fact, to live as a sort of like roving sort of like man who makes his own rules, you have to have a kind of a code.
